title: PepsiCo – Strategic Supply Chain Planning Amid Macroeconomic Uncertainty
gist: A Capital Economics marketing case study describing how PepsiCo bought customized macro/commodity scenario forecasts (base/upside/downside across oil, gas, grains, sugar, aluminium, vegetable oils and ~9 regions) to inform internal commodity price guidance and long-range supply chain planning. It is promotional, with no specific forecasts, numbers, or market views disclosed.

insights.claim: PepsiCo uses external macro/commodity scenario forecasts (base, upside, downside) as a direct input into its internal commodity price guidance and long-range supply chain strategy.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: Signals that large consumer-staples firms formalize commodity hedging/planning around third-party scenario analysis; watch PEP margins as a tell on input-cost forecasting accuracy.
insights.quote: Our research directly informed PepsiCo’s commodity price guidance and long-range supply chain strategy.

insights.claim: The commodities flagged as critical to PepsiCo's supply chain are oil, gas, grains, sugar, aluminium, and vegetable oils.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: These six inputs are the cost-driver basket to track for PEP packaged-food/beverage margin pressure; aluminium and vegetable oils are less obvious watch items.
insights.quote: commodities critical to PepsiCo’s supply chain, such as oil, gas, grains, sugar, aluminium, and vegetable oils

insights.claim: PepsiCo's macro risk monitoring spans Brazil, Canada, China, EU, India, Mexico, Russia, the UK, and the US, plus trade-policy and election-outcome scenarios.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: Indicates which EM/DM jurisdictions and political risks a global staples operator deems material to operations; trade-policy and election risk are explicit planning variables.
insights.quote: We also evaluated trade policy risks and potential election outcomes.
